---
title: "Intro to Mathematical Software"
subtitle: "Public course resources"
toc: false
---

This site collects public-facing resources for **Intro to Mathematical
Software** — a hands-on tour of the tools mathematicians actually use:
**LaTeX, R, Quarto**, and **generative AI assistants used carefully**.
Curated by [Matt Hester](https://matthewhester.com).

It is a **curated resource site**, not a raw course archive. Anything
section- or roster-specific lives in the LMS; this site is what stays
public.

## Where to start

- [Syllabus](syllabus.qmd) — course goals, structure, and policies
  (sanitized for public view)
- [Schedule](schedule.qmd) — generic week-by-week topic map
- [Notes](notes/index.qmd) — short conceptual write-ups
- [Labs](labs/index.qmd) — step-by-step walkthroughs you can run on
  your own machine
- [Assignments](assignments/index.qmd) — homework and project briefs
- [Examples](examples/index.qmd) — small, reproducible code examples
- [Resources](resources/index.qmd) — software setup, AI guidelines,
  CAS options, and outside links

## What this course is about

Three skills, woven together:

1. **Write mathematics well** in LaTeX.
2. **Compute, visualize, and simulate** with R.
3. **Make your work reproducible** using Quarto.

Plus a serious unit on **using AI assistants responsibly** — what they
help with, what they get wrong, and how to verify and acknowledge.

## What this course is not

It is not a survey of every mathematical software package. The optional
**[CAS options page](resources/cas-options.qmd)** points at SageMath,
WolframAlpha, Python + SymPy, MATLAB/Octave, and Maple for students
who want to explore a second computational tool.
